segments.title: The Ripon II's redesigned engine installation
segments.summary: The Ripon II was so extensively revised around its engine and cooling system that it barely resembled the earlier Ripon I.
segments.body: The Ripon II's nose was reshaped around a fully cowled 570-horsepower Napier Lion engine and a larger two-bladed wooden airscrew. Removing the earlier nose-mounted radiator shutters gave the aircraft a pointed profile that the propeller spinner emphasized. Cooling instead came from quadrant-shaped retractable radiators that the pilot could extend from both sides of the fuselage behind the engine bay. The arrangement shows how a revised engine installation could transform the appearance and cooling system of an otherwise related torpedo-bomber design.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the Ripon II with the earlier aircraft and describes its Napier Lion installation, cowlings, propeller, and retractable side radiators.

segments.title: An undercarriage built around torpedo clearance
segments.summary: The Ripon II combined a larger balanced rudder with a new bent-axle undercarriage that left room beneath the fuselage for a torpedo.
segments.body: The redesign extended beyond the Ripon II's nose and cooling system. A taller balanced rudder with substantially greater area changed the aircraft's control surfaces. Each half of the new undercarriage used a bent axle attached to the lower longerons, while telescopic oleo-pneumatic legs ran out to fittings on the lower centre-section front spar. Routing those structural members around the fuselage left the central space clear for the torpedo, making the landing gear part of the aircraft's weapon-carrying arrangement rather than an isolated feature.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the enlarged rudder, the redesigned bent-axle undercarriage, its oleo-pneumatic legs, and the clearance left for a torpedo.

segments.title: A modern exterior over a familiar folding airframe
segments.summary: Behind the refined exterior, the Ripon II retained a mixed steel-and-wood structure and Blackburn's established folding-wing arrangement for shipboard use.
segments.body: Despite its refined exterior, the Ripon II retained the basic fuselage architecture of its predecessors and was stressed for catapult launches. The airframe separated into three detachable units: a steel engine mounting, a steel-tube centre structure, and a tie-rod-braced wooden rear fuselage. Its swept-back single-bay wings mixed spruce spars and ribs with metal compression members and streamlined steel interplane struts. Hinges at the rear spars let both upper and lower wings fold aft in Blackburn's established shipborne arrangement, while ailerons on all four wings followed the company's standard practice. Together, the construction details show how carrier stowage and launch requirements shaped the aircraft beneath its updated outer form.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the inherited fuselage structure, composite wing construction, and wings folding back like earlier Blackburn shipborne aircraft.

segments.title: Fuel arrangements for a long reconnaissance sortie
segments.summary: The Ripon II placed its main fuel tank at the centre of gravity and could trade armament for an auxiliary tank that extended reconnaissance endurance to fourteen hours.
segments.body: The Ripon II carried a 145-gallon main fuel tank at the aircraft's centre of gravity and pumped fuel onward to a 17-gallon service tank. A separate four-gallon coolant tank sat beside the service tank in the upper centre section. For reconnaissance duties, the aircraft could remove its armament and suspend a 120-gallon auxiliary tank from the torpedo crutches, extending endurance to fourteen hours. A wind-driven pump built into the auxiliary tank's nose maintained flow into the main fuel system. The arrangement makes the operational trade-off explicit: the same mounting space could support either a torpedo strike or the fuel needed for prolonged scouting.

evidence.note: The transcript locates the fuel and coolant tanks and explains that an auxiliary tank on the torpedo crutches could raise reconnaissance endurance to fourteen hours.
